The same pattern holds for the other failure modes. Erlang mailboxes are better behaved than Go channels in some ways: a mailbox has one reader, and sends are asynchronous. But they are still shared mutable communication mechanisms, and they still inherit familiar problems.

Mailbox growth is the clearest operational example. Erlang mailboxes are unbounded and provide no built-in backpressure. If a process receives messages faster than it can process them, the queue grows until memory pressure takes the node down. Fred Hébert’s `pobox` exists specifically because production Erlang systems hit this issue often enough to need dedicated tooling. The article uses that as evidence that this is not a theoretical edge case but a recurring systems problem.

Races also survive the move to actors. Messages from multiple senders can interleave nondeterministically, which creates ordering races the language does not prevent. And messages are dynamically typed, so any process can send any Erlang term to any other process with no compile-time guarantee that the receiver expects that shape. OTP behaviors and conventions help structure protocols, but they are not type-level enforcement.

This is a recurring theme in the article: Erlang reduces surface area for bugs, but it does not make those bugs structurally impossible.

### Erlang’s reliability comes from mitigations, and mitigations have a cost

The article is careful not to dismiss Erlang’s ecosystem. It explicitly says Erlang has good answers to these problems.

For deadlocks and call cycles, teams are taught to prefer asynchronous casts where possible and to rely on timeouts. For mailbox blowups, they monitor queue sizes, add backpressure manually, or use libraries like `pobox`. For race-like interleavings, they design protocols carefully and test them aggressively. For protocol mismatches, they lean on OTP behaviors, code review, and team discipline.

These practices are a major reason Erlang systems are as robust as they are. But the author’s complaint is about where the guarantees live. Most of them live in convention, design rules, operations playbooks, and runtime monitoring. They are not enforced by the language or compiler. Some cannot even be checked until the system is under real production load.

That shifts the burden onto teams. A developer has to learn not just Erlang syntax or OTP APIs, but which conventions are load-bearing, which patterns look innocent but are unsafe, and which runtime signals must never be ignored. The article names this the “discipline tax.” It can absolutely work, especially in experienced teams with stable codebases. But it is fragile over time. Team turnover, uneven expertise, and operational drift slowly erode convention-based safety.

### Isolation also creates a throughput bottleneck

Even if a team does everything right, the article argues that the actor-isolation model has a built-in performance constraint.

A process owns its state, and all access to that state must go through its mailbox. That means one process, one mailbox, one message at a time. Under moderate load, this is a reasonable way to serialize updates and preserve local invariants. Under heavy read sharing, it becomes a funnel.

The article uses examples like routing tables, configuration stores, session registries, and lookup caches. If a thousand processes need to read some shared data and the pure model says “ask the owner process and wait,” then the owner becomes the bottleneck. Throughput falls because the model serializes access by design.

This is not presented as an implementation accident. It is the direct price of safety-through-isolation. If nobody else can touch your state, then everyone who needs it must line up. In that sense, performance pressure is not incidental to the model. It follows from the model.

### ETS and later features are deliberate escape hatches from the pure model

The most interesting part of the story is what Erlang did next. It did not stay pure.

ETS, Erlang Term Storage, exists because mailbox serialization is too expensive for many real workloads. ETS puts mutable in-memory tables outside the process model so any process can read or write without asking an owner process first. That is a conscious engineering trade-off, not an accidental compromise.

The same pressure led to more specialized mechanisms. OTP 21 added `persistent_term`, aimed at values that are read constantly and written rarely, such as configuration, routing tables, or compiled regular expressions. OTP 22 added `atomics` and `counters`, which expose direct shared-memory style operations without message passing or process mediation. Each step moved further away from pure actor isolation because each solved a performance problem the previous mechanism could not address cheaply enough.

The article is precise about the semantics. ETS does copy terms on read and write. Individual key operations are atomic, but multi-key operations are not. Traversals do not provide snapshot consistency. None of these facilities participate in the ownership and mailbox semantics that define Erlang’s isolation story.

That is the core argument: once performance matters enough, Erlang reaches for shared mutable mechanisms outside the actor model. And when it does, the classic shared-state bugs return.

### The bugs found in OTP show where the model leaks

To make that concrete, the article cites work by Maria Christakis and Konstantinos Sagonas, who built a static race detector for Erlang and integrated it into Dialyzer. They ran it against OTP itself, which matters because OTP is not toy code. It is heavily tested, widely deployed infrastructure used across the Erlang ecosystem.

They found previously unknown race conditions, and the article highlights that these were not exotic one-off curiosities. They appeared in familiar categories exactly where isolation had been relaxed.

One category was ETS races. A process reads a value, decides what to do, and then another process updates the table before the first one writes back. That is classic check-then-act or TOCTOU. Another category was process registration races. Erlang’s global process-name registry is mutable shared state, so the usual namespace races apply: two processes compete to register a name, or one process looks up a name and sends a message just as the named process dies and the name is rebound. The third category was process dictionary races, involving Erlang’s per-process mutable dictionary and the subtle ordering dependencies it can create.

What matters is not that Erlang has some exotic failure mode no other language has. It is almost the opposite. Once the system steps outside strict isolation, it rediscovers the same old concurrency bugs: TOCTOU, non-atomic multi-step updates, and races on global mutable namespaces.

### The broader pattern is that isolation helps, but does not finish the job

The article closes by zooming out. Erlang gives the actor model its strongest practical expression: isolated heaps, copied messages, mailbox ownership, and battle-tested reliability patterns. Those choices do help. They narrow the concurrency surface and make some classes of corruption much harder.

But they do not provide the complete escape from concurrency complexity that the isolation narrative promises. The communication layer still behaves like shared mutable state in important ways. Deadlock freedom does not compose. Backpressure is not automatic. Ordering races remain. Protocol correctness is mostly discipline. And when the performance cost of strict isolation becomes too high, the language introduces shared mutable escape hatches like ETS, `persistent_term`, `atomics`, and `counters`.

So the story is not that Erlang failed. It is that Erlang, despite being the best version of the idea, reveals the limits of the idea. Isolation is powerful and useful, but not sufficient. It reduces some risks, shifts others, and eventually runs into a wall where practical systems need more direct forms of sharing. That, for the author, is the real lesson.

articles.title: A Survival Guide to a PhD (2016)
articles.title_display: Karpathy’s Practical Heuristics for Surviving a CS PhD
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway
Karpathy’s guide frames a PhD as a high-autonomy, high-variance apprenticeship in research that can be deeply rewarding, but only if you choose it deliberately, understand the incentives around you, and navigate advisers, projects, and your own psychology with clear eyes.

## Body

### The PhD is not just more school, it is a very particular kind of life

Karpathy opens by refusing to sell the PhD as an obviously good choice. That is the right starting point. His argument is not that everyone should do one, but that the experience has a specific shape, and you should want that shape before you sign up for five or more years of it.

What makes the PhD attractive is not mainly the credential. It is the mix of freedom, ownership, and depth. You get unusual autonomy over what you learn and what problems you pursue. The work you produce is attached to your name in a way that often feels more personal than work inside a large company. You also get a rare chance to become genuinely expert at something narrow and difficult, right at the edge of what is known.

He also emphasizes softer but still real forces: status, the density of smart peers, and the option value of the degree. In his view, a PhD tends to preserve future choices better than skipping it does. You can move from PhD to industry fairly easily, but the reverse path is harder, especially if your long-term goal involves research or academia. Even outside academia, he argues, the training can make you more attractive to employers and potentially land you in more interesting roles.

But this is only half the story. The other half is the disclaimer, and it is unusually blunt. A PhD can involve long periods of unstructured work, discarded efforts, low pay relative to friends in industry, paper deadlines that consume your life, and repeated doubt about whether any of it matters. Research means uncertainty by default. You may spend months on an idea that goes nowhere. You may publish work that gets little attention. If you need external structure, clear progress metrics, or frequent reinforcement, this environment can be rough. His advice is to lean slightly negative if you are unsure, and ideally get a taste of research first so you know what kind of pain and satisfaction you are signing up for.

### Getting in is mostly about evidence that you can already do research

Once the decision to pursue a PhD is made, Karpathy’s guidance gets very practical. The main input to a strong application is not grades and not even publications in isolation. It is reference letters, especially letters from respected researchers who can say concrete things about your initiative, independence, and ability to generate ideas and carry them through.

That distinction matters. A generic letter saying you did well in class is weak because it speaks to coursework, not to research potential. A strong letter says you are exceptional among students the writer has actually supervised. That is the signal admissions committees care about most.

This leads to a simple but important reallocation of effort. Karpathy says he spent too much undergraduate energy optimizing grades when more of that time should have gone into research or at least serious personal projects. The ideal path is to get involved in research early, preferably under multiple supervisors, because you will need several strong letters. A publication from a summer research project is a major plus, but not strictly required if the letters are strong. Grades matter mostly in the sense that they should not be bad enough to create concern.

He also warns against a common failure mode: trying too hard to impress potential advisers through unsolicited emails or aggressive conference interactions. Professors are busy, and that strategy often creates more irritation than opportunity. The durable way in is to do good work near people who can evaluate it firsthand.

### Choosing a school is really about the local feedback loop

Karpathy’s advice on selecting a PhD program is less about rankings as prestige theater and more about what top environments do to your day-to-day development. A strong school creates positive feedback loops. It attracts strong students and faculty, and those people become your collaborators, critics, and friends. That concentration of talent compounds over time.

Still, school choice should not collapse into “pick the highest-ranked place.” He stresses two other constraints. First, the program should have several advisers you could realistically work with, not just one dream match. That redundancy is a safety mechanism. Advisers may leave, become unavailable, turn out to be a poor fit, or simply not have room. If your entire plan depends on one person, you are taking unnecessary risk.

Second, the physical environment matters more than new admits often realize. You are not choosing only a lab. You are choosing a place to live through a large chunk of your twenties or early thirties. The local quality of life, the city, the social environment, and the basic feel of the place all matter because a PhD is long enough that those factors become structural, not cosmetic.

### The adviser relationship is the central system you have to understand

The strongest section of the guide is about advisers. Karpathy treats the student-adviser relationship as the defining force in the PhD experience, and he argues that students do better when they stop seeing it as mysterious and instead understand it as a real incentive system.

An adviser is not just a mentor. They are also a professional actor with career goals, funding constraints, departmental politics, and evaluation pressures. If you understand how your adviser gets tenure, funding, recognition, and influence, many confusing behaviors become legible. This does not reduce the relationship to pure transaction, but it does help explain why certain requests, priorities, or publication pressures exist.

One of his most useful heuristics is the distinction between pre-tenure and post-tenure faculty. Younger professors often have stronger incentives to publish aggressively and may be much more hands-on. They may comment on equations, propose concrete projects, read or even write code, and stay closer to implementation details. That can be energizing and educational, but it can also be intense because their need for output maps directly onto your workload.

Senior faculty often supervise at a higher level of abstraction. They may have larger groups and more non-research commitments, so their guidance may look less like line-by-line technical correction and more like strategic advice: what literature to read, how to frame the work, whom to talk to, how to position the paper. The low-level support in those labs often comes from senior students or postdocs. Neither model is inherently better. They simply produce different PhD experiences.

He broadens this into a set of adviser “axes”: personal warmth versus distance, hands-on versus hands-off style, model-centric versus problem-centric research taste, frequent versus sparse meetings, responsiveness, expectations around hours, willingness to fund equipment and conference travel, openness to internships, and the theory-to-application spectrum. The point is not to score advisers abstractly. It is to figure out where they sit in this space and whether that matches how you work and what you want to become.

### Fit is discovered socially, not from official descriptions

Because adviser choice is so consequential, Karpathy recommends treating it with the seriousness of a long-term partnership. You should absolutely talk to potential advisers directly, but formal conversations are only one source of information. More revealing data often comes from current students, especially in casual settings where they are more likely to be candid.

This is a subtle point. Publicly, students may hesitate to criticize their adviser or may answer in vague terms. Informally, with specific questions and the right context, you can learn much more: how often the adviser meets students, whether they give timely feedback, whether they protect students politically, whether they create pressure around weekends, whether they support internships, and what kinds of students tend to thrive or struggle in the group.

This matters because an adviser is not just selecting projects for you. They are shaping the operating system of your research life. The wrong fit can make even a strong topic miserable. The right fit can make difficult work sustainable.

### The deeper lesson is that surviving a PhD is mostly about managing uncertainty

Even in the visible parts of the article, a larger theme emerges: the PhD is a constant exercise in uncertainty management. There is uncertainty in whether you should do one at all, uncertainty in admissions, uncertainty in adviser fit, and uncertainty in research itself. Karpathy’s advice consistently tries to reduce avoidable uncertainty and help you tolerate the rest.

That is why he keeps returning to choice-preserving moves. Do research early so you know what you are getting into. Prioritize references because they are the best signal of research readiness. Pick a school with multiple adviser options. Learn how your adviser’s incentives work so their behavior is not opaque. Build your understanding of the environment around you instead of drifting through it.

There is also a psychological throughline. A PhD rewards people who can operate without much external structure, absorb failed months without collapsing, and keep going when progress is ambiguous. Karpathy does not romanticize that. He presents it almost as a prerequisite. The degree is valuable partly because it forces rapid personal growth, but that growth is purchased through stress, ambiguity, and repeated self-correction.

### In the end, the PhD is worth it only if you value the thing itself

Karpathy closes the circle by rejecting the idea that a PhD is only justified as a path to academia. His view is that the experience has intrinsic value. It can be worth doing because of the freedom, the intellectual depth, the people, and the chance to spend a few years trying to discover something new.

That does not mean the trade-offs disappear. You still give up money, certainty, and often lifestyle comfort. You still risk investing enormous effort into work that may look small from the outside. But if you are motivated by exploration, ownership, and becoming truly good at hard, open-ended problems, those sacrifices can make sense.

So the guide’s real message is not “do a PhD.” It is more precise than that. A PhD can be one of the best environments for becoming an independent researcher and for growing quickly as a person, but it is only survivable if you enter for the right reasons, choose the surrounding people carefully, and expect the difficulty rather than being surprised by it.

articles.title: Emacs and Vim in the Age of AI
articles.title_display: How AI Changes the Case for Emacs and Vim
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway
AI raises real competitive pressure from VS Code and AI-first editors, but it may also remove the biggest historical barriers to Emacs and Vim—configuration, extension, troubleshooting, and terminal-native workflows—making their future more resilient than the usual doom narratives suggest.

## Body

### A long-time editor partisan looks at AI without pretending nothing changed

Bozhidar Batsov frames the question personally and honestly. He is not a neutral observer. He has spent more than 20 years deep in Emacs, maintaining popular packages, contributing to Emacs itself, and treating the editor as both tool and hobby. At the same time, he has recently been relearning Vim and Neovim from scratch, partly to compare how the two ecosystems solve similar problems. And like most of the industry, he has also been experimenting with AI coding tools, especially Claude Code.

That combination gives the article its shape. This is not a hot take about “AI killing editors.” It is a working engineer asking what AI means for two editor cultures built around power-user workflows, extensibility, and years of accumulated craft. His answer is neither complacent nor apocalyptic. Emacs and Vim are not obviously doomed, but the world around them is changing in ways that matter.

### The biggest threat is not AI itself, but where AI is being productized

The first risk is distribution and product gravity. VS Code is already the dominant editor, and Batsov expects it to remain the default landing zone for first-class AI integrations: Copilot, Codex, Claude, Gemini, and whatever comes next. Microsoft has both the incentive and the resources to make VS Code the best host for AI-assisted development.

Then there are AI-native editors such as Cursor and Windsurf. Their core advantage is not simply “they have an LLM panel.” They are designed around AI workflows from the start: context management, inline diffs, multi-file edits, and agent loops feel native instead of bolted on. That matters because once a workflow is architected around agent interaction, the editor is no longer just a text manipulation environment. It becomes an orchestration layer for AI.

For Emacs and Vim, that is dangerous in a very practical sense. Every developer who moves into one of those ecosystems is one less person learning Emacs Lisp, Vim motions, Lua plugin APIs, or the social norms of those communities. Batsov calls this gravity well real. He notes that he personally avoided Cursor and Windsurf because they are effectively VS Code forks and he dislikes VS Code, but the broader market trend still stands.

### AI also attacks the classic argument for power editors

Emacs and Vim have historically justified their steep learning curves with a performance argument. You invest in keybindings, macros, text objects, composable commands, and extensibility, and over time you become much faster at editing code.

AI changes that premise. If the machine is writing much of the code, the bottleneck shifts. The scarce skill is no longer pure mechanical editing speed. It becomes the ability to express intent clearly, steer generation, review diffs, and judge output quality. That is a different optimization target.

This is why the traditional “spend six months learning Emacs and you’ll be 10x faster” pitch gets weaker. In a world where a junior developer can generate a large application scaffold in an afternoon using a tool like Cursor, the return on mastering a complex editor is less obvious. Batsov does not claim the old skills are useless, but he does argue that the center of gravity may move away from raw editing throughput.

He also points out a structural disadvantage: corporate backing. VS Code has Microsoft. Cursor has venture funding. Emacs depends on volunteers and the FSF. Vim lost Bram Moolenaar and is now community-maintained. Neovim has a small, dedicated core team. AI integration moves quickly and depends on evolving APIs, models, and product patterns. Well-funded teams can assign full-time engineers to keep up. Volunteer projects move at the pace of spare time.

At the far edge of the argument sits the doomsday scenario: if AI agents eventually take specifications and produce tested, deployed software without human intervention, coding editors of every kind become less relevant, not just Emacs and Vim. Batsov does not think that is likely in the near term, but he thinks it is intellectually honest to name the possibility.

### The same AI wave may remove the hardest part of Emacs and Vim adoption

After laying out the risks, the article turns in a more interesting direction. Batsov argues that AI may help Emacs and Vim precisely where they have always been weakest.

The first place is troubleshooting. These editors are powerful, but they are also famous for steep learning curves and miserable error messages. In Emacs, something like `Wrong type argument: stringp, nil` can be enough to send a user back to a mainstream IDE. AI is unusually good at bridging that gap. It can inspect an init file, explain cryptic failures, identify a broken keybinding, and walk a user through a fix in plain language.

His point is not that AI makes the editors simpler. It makes them more teachable. Instead of spending hours searching old mailing list threads and half-relevant blog posts, users can now interact with a patient guide that understands both the error and the surrounding configuration. Batsov says he rarely needs this help in Emacs anymore, but he has found it useful while working in Neovim, where he has less accumulated expertise.

He cites a concrete example of someone returning to Emacs after years away because Claude Code made the configuration burden manageable again. That matters because it suggests AI can do more than attract new users. It can also reactivate lapsed ones by lowering the maintenance tax that previously pushed them out.

### The real unlock is not chat inside the editor, but plain-English customization

Batsov thinks the most under-discussed effect of AI is on extensibility. Emacs and Vim have long been held back by the fact that serious customization requires learning unfamiliar languages. Emacs Lisp is a niche Lisp dialect from the 1980s. VimScript has never been approachable. Neovim’s move to Lua helped, but Lua is still not a language most developers already know.

That language barrier, in his view, has been a bigger constraint than the editors themselves. The problem was never lack of power. It was that the path to harnessing that power required learning an obscure extension language just to make the editor behave the way you wanted. Many users stopped at copy-pasting snippets from README files.

AI changes that dynamic. You can now describe behavior in English and get usable Emacs Lisp, VimScript, or Lua back. Need an Emacs function to reflow a paragraph to 72 columns with a prefix? Need a `lazy.nvim` setup with LSP bindings? AI can draft it. That does not eliminate the need for understanding, but it dramatically lowers the threshold for effective customization.

He extends the same argument to plugin development and maintenance. In the Emacs community, he estimates that perhaps 50 to 100 people drive most of the meaningful progress. That concentration is a strength in expertise but a weakness in ecosystem resilience. The C internals of Emacs, Vim, and Neovim are maintained by even fewer people, and old C codebases are not attracting a growing pool of contributors.

AI helps on two fronts. It can make newcomers productive in unfamiliar implementation languages, and it can make existing maintainers faster at the unglamorous parts of the job. Batsov specifically calls out test scaffolding, documentation, and package maintenance as areas where AI has already been valuable for him.

### Emacs and Neovim are not standing still

The article is careful not to present Emacs and Neovim as passive victims. There is already a meaningful ecosystem of AI integrations.

On the Emacs side, Batsov lists `gptel`, a flexible LLM client that supports multiple backends including Claude, GPT, Gemini, and local models; `ellama`, which works through `llama.cpp` and Ollama; `aider.el` for integrating the Aider pair-programming tool; `copilot.el`, which he currently maintains; `elysium` for AI-assisted coding with inline diff application; `agent-shell`, which connects Emacs buffers to agent tools such as Claude Code and Gemini CLI through the Agent Client Protocol; and ECA, an editor-agnostic assistant built in Clojure that uses a protocol conceptually similar to LSP and works with Emacs and Neovim.

On the Neovim side, he points to `avante.nvim`, which aims for a Cursor-like in-editor experience; `codecompanion.nvim`, a multi-provider alternative in the Copilot Chat space; `copilot.lua` for native Copilot support; and `gp.nvim` for ChatGPT-style sessions with multiple providers.

The larger point is architectural. Building these integrations is not as hard as many people assume. The APIs are usually straightforward, and both editors are highly programmable. Their extensibility makes it possible to integrate AI in ways that feel editor-native. If anything, AI may accelerate plugin development further by helping developers build those integrations faster.

### Terminal-native agents may fit these editors better than polished AI GUIs do

One of the strongest observations in the article is also the simplest: many of today’s most capable AI coding tools are terminal-first. Claude Code, Aider, and several Copilot CLI experiences run in the terminal. Emacs and Vim already live comfortably there.

That creates an unexpectedly strong fit. Running Claude Code in an Emacs `vterm` buffer or in a Neovim terminal split is not a workaround. It is a coherent workflow. The AI agent sits in one pane, the source in another, and the rest of the environment remains yours: your keybindings, window management, shell tools, and editor habits. Instead of accepting a monolithic AI editor, you compose your own stack.

Batsov sees that composability as an advantage over GUI editors where the AI system is tightly coupled to one interface model. A terminal-native agent plus a programmable editor gives the user more freedom to mix and match tools. He adds a nice irony here: if programming increasingly becomes prompt writing, then a powerful text editor is still valuable, because prompts are text too. Even the act of steering an agent benefits from good editing tools.

He also notes a rough edge in current AI tooling. Claude Code, which he otherwise likes, does not use readline, so his Emacs keybindings do not work properly in it, and its Vim emulation is poor. That complaint reinforces his larger theme: interface details still matter, even in AI-heavy workflows.

### The likely future is not extinction, but adaptation

The story the article tells is ultimately about asymmetry. AI undeniably strengthens the position of already dominant tools like VS Code and gives AI-first editors a compelling product narrative. It also weakens some traditional arguments for investing deeply in editors whose value proposition was built around manual editing efficiency.

But the same technology also attacks several of the oldest and most painful friction points in Emacs and Vim: inscrutable errors, hard-to-learn extension languages, and a thin contributor base stretched across decades-old code and plugin ecosystems. If AI makes customization, contribution, and support easier, then these editors may become more accessible without losing their character.

That is why Batsov refuses both simplistic conclusions. The future is not “nothing changes,” and it is not “Emacs and Vim are dead.” The more plausible outcome is that these editors survive by leaning into what they already do well: programmability, composability, and terminal affinity. In the age of AI, those may turn out to be more durable advantages than they first appear.

articles.title: Games with loot boxes to get minimum 16 age rating across Europe
articles.title_display: PEGI Raises Loot Box Games to 16+ in Europe
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway
Starting in June, PEGI will automatically rate games with paid random-item mechanics at 16+ across Europe, marking a policy shift that treats loot boxes less as a minor content warning and more as a potentially harmful gambling-adjacent feature.

## Body

### PEGI is changing how Europe classifies loot boxes

The article centers on a straightforward but significant change: from June, the Pan-European Game Information system, or PEGI, will give games with loot boxes a minimum age rating of 16.

That matters because PEGI is the age-ratings body used across much of Europe, including the UK. Until now, games with loot boxes could still receive lower age ratings as long as their broader content fit those categories. PEGI already flagged such mechanics with a label warning players that the game contains paid random items. The new rule goes further. Instead of treating loot boxes as just another disclosure, PEGI is now making them a rating floor.

In practical terms, if a game includes loot boxes, it will no longer be able to land in PEGI’s younger age bands. The policy applies across Europe, not just the UK, even though the headline emphasizes the British market.

### The trigger is concern about gambling-like design

The reasoning is not subtle. PEGI says loot boxes can “encourage and normalise gambling-like behaviour.” That framing is the core of the decision.

Loot boxes are digital purchases where players spend money without knowing exactly what they will receive. The random reward structure is what keeps drawing scrutiny from regulators, researchers, and campaigners. PEGI’s change reflects a view that the issue is not only monetization, but the way uncertainty and paid chance-based rewards interact, especially for younger players.

The article ties this back to wider public pressure around children and gambling exposure. It cites criticism from campaigners and long-running concerns that these mechanics blur the line between game spending and gambling behavior. PEGI is effectively acknowledging that concern in its ratings model.

### This is a stronger intervention than the old “paid random items” label

One of the important details in the story is that PEGI is not starting from zero. It already had a content descriptor for paid random items. That label told buyers and parents that a game included chance-based purchases.

But labels and age gates do different jobs. A label depends on someone noticing it and interpreting it correctly. A rating floor changes storefront placement, parental controls, and marketability. It is a much more opinionated piece of policy.

That trade-off seems intentional. PEGI appears to have concluded that disclosure alone was not enough. If the concern is normalization of gambling-like systems, then a passive warning does less than an explicit age restriction. The shift from descriptor to minimum age rating is the real story here.

### The industry response is likely to be about design choices, not just marketing

Although the article is short, the implication for developers and publishers is clear. If a game is targeting younger audiences in Europe, loot boxes now become an architectural product decision, not just a monetization feature.

A studio that wants to stay below PEGI 16 will need to avoid mechanics that count as paid random items. That could push teams toward battle passes, direct-purchase cosmetic stores, fixed-price bundles, or other monetization systems where the buyer knows what they are getting. In other words, the rating policy can shape design upstream.

This is why the change lands differently from a warning sticker. Ratings affect distribution and audience reach. For games built around broad family or teen markets, the cost of keeping loot boxes may now be higher than the revenue they generate.

### The broader debate is still unresolved

The article does not claim Europe has reached a universal legal consensus that loot boxes are gambling. It stops short of that. Instead, PEGI is making a classification judgment based on harm and behavioral risk.

That distinction matters. PEGI is a ratings organization, not a legislature. Its new rule does not ban loot boxes. It does not redefine them in law. What it does is encode a stronger cultural and consumer-protection signal into the age-rating system.

So the move sits in the middle ground between disclosure and prohibition. Games can still ship with loot boxes. They just carry a 16+ consequence if they do.

### The outcome is a clearer, stricter line for European game releases

The final outcome is a simpler rule for parents, storefronts, and developers: if a game includes loot boxes, PEGI will classify it at 16 or above starting in June.

That makes the system easier to understand, but also more consequential for studios. PEGI is saying the presence of paid random rewards is itself enough to move a game into an older category, regardless of whether the rest of the content might otherwise qualify for a lower rating.

For players and families, that is a stronger warning. For developers, it is a new constraint. And for the broader games industry, it is another sign that random monetization mechanics are being treated less like standard free-to-play design and more like a risk factor that demands explicit limits.

articles.title: An investigation of the forces behind the age-verification bills
articles.title_display: Investigation Ties Meta to Age-Verification Bill Push
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway
The article points to Meta as the central force behind a wave of US age-verification bills, arguing that the campaign is less about child safety than about building device- and OS-level control and surveillance while shielding Meta’s own platforms from equivalent new obligations.

## Body

### Following the money behind the age-verification push

LWN’s piece is short, but the story it highlights is not. It centers on a Reddit investigation by user `Ok_Lingonberry3296`, who dug through public records to answer a simple question: who is actually driving the recent surge of age-verification bills in US state legislatures?

The answer, according to the cited investigation, is Meta.

What makes the investigation notable is the kind of evidence it uses. This is not framed as rumor or vibes-based attribution. The researcher says they worked from IRS 990 filings, Senate lobbying disclosures, state ethics databases, campaign-finance records, corporate registries, WHOIS data, and Wayback Machine archives. The resulting picture is described as a coordinated influence operation, one that uses “child safety” as the public narrative while pushing for infrastructure that would move age verification down into operating systems and devices.

That framing matters. Once age checks move from an app or website into the OS layer, the policy stops being just a content rule. It starts looking like a platform-control and identity-surveillance problem.

### The core claim: Meta is using intermediaries to shape the laws

The strongest details come from the quoted material in the comments. One commenter pulls out the numbers and names directly: Meta spent $26.3 million on federal lobbying in 2025 and used more than 86 lobbyists across 45 states. The investigation also claims Meta funded a nationally active advocacy group called DCA that does not appear to have a legal existence in the IRS system.

The mechanics of the campaign are a big part of the story. According to the summary quoted on LWN, Meta hired Hilltop Public Solutions both to run its $45 million super PAC and to coordinate DCA’s messaging. That overlap is presented as evidence that this was not a loose ecosystem of aligned interests, but a tightly managed messaging operation.

The article also ties the current campaign to an earlier documented Meta tactic: hiring Targeted Victory to run an astroturfing campaign against TikTok, again using child safety as the rhetorical frame. That historical comparison is important because it turns the current allegations into a pattern rather than a one-off. The claim is not just that Meta benefits from these bills. It is that Meta has previously been publicly documented using outside firms and nominally independent groups to manufacture concern in ways that serve its commercial interests while obscuring its own role.

### Why age verification is such a loaded technical choice

The LWN note links to an earlier article on the California law, which gives context for why Linux users and open-source developers care so much. The concern is not simply that websites might ask users for proof of age. The deeper concern is that lawmakers, influenced by large platforms, may end up requiring verification at the operating-system or app-store level.

That is a very different architecture.

At the app level, age checks are annoying, invasive, and often ineffective. At the OS level, they become part of the trust model of the device itself. They create pressure for stronger identity binding, tighter app distribution controls, and fewer ways for users to run unapproved software. In other words, age verification can become a policy justification for expanding the same locked-down platform features that already benefit dominant mobile vendors.

The Reddit investigator’s privacy argument, as quoted by LWN, is exactly this: these bills are helping build surveillance infrastructure at the operating-system level, while the company behind the push does not face comparable new requirements for its own services.

### The technical argument in the comments: this is hard to enforce, but not equally hard everywhere

The comments on LWN add an interesting engineering debate. One commenter argues that age verification “can’t work” because it is really an “access in private” problem. The point is straightforward: if a child is old enough, or has help, many software-based restrictions can be bypassed. On Linux, for example, creating another account could be enough in some cases. More generally, reinstalling an OS or using a different device can defeat local restrictions.

That is the optimistic open-systems view.

Another commenter pushes back and says the scheme absolutely can be made difficult to evade on proprietary platforms. If bootloaders are locked, installing untrusted software or replacing the operating system becomes unrealistic for most people. Apple, Google, Samsung, and similar vendors already have incentives to maintain that control, and age-verification mandates could reinforce it. The commenter’s point is not that the systems are perfect, but that they can be hardened enough that ordinary users cannot practically bypass them.

That exchange gets to the real technical trade-off. On open systems, these mandates are brittle and intrusive. On closed systems, they are intrusive and potentially much more enforceable. So the likely long-term result is not a clean safety mechanism. It is stronger pressure toward locked-down devices, trusted execution paths, and centralized gatekeepers.

### This is also a political story about influence, not just a policy story about minors

Several comments widen the frame beyond technology. One points to the familiar post-Citizens United criticism: the legal structure of modern US political spending makes it easier for corporations to shape legislation through lobbying, PACs, and “outside” advocacy groups. Another notes that Meta is active in EU lobbying as well, connecting the US campaign to a broader pattern of trying to influence digital regulation in multiple jurisdictions.

Even if those comments are broader than the article itself, they reinforce the core message of the investigation: age-verification legislation should not be read at face value. The public justification is child safety, but the operational machinery behind it appears to be conventional power politics—money, intermediaries, message control, and pressure applied across many legislatures at once.

That matters because these laws are often discussed as if they were straightforward responses to social harms. The investigation instead suggests they are strategic interventions by an incumbent platform with specific commercial incentives.

### The uncomfortable conclusion: the proposed cure may be worse than the stated disease

The most striking thing in the LWN piece is not just that Meta is allegedly behind the push. It is the asymmetry. The company most associated with large-scale engagement systems and youth-safety criticism is, according to the investigation, promoting laws that push verification and control downward into operating systems and devices rather than imposing equivalent burdens on its own platforms.

That is a clever move if you are thinking in terms of incentives. OS-level requirements externalize cost. They spread implementation burdens across device makers, platform vendors, app ecosystems, and open-source projects. They also create barriers that are easier for large incumbents to navigate than for smaller services or independent software communities.

So the article lands less as a scandal-of-the-week and more as a warning about where regulation is heading. Age verification is being sold as a narrow protective measure. But the evidence collected by the Reddit investigator, and amplified by LWN, suggests a broader project: normalize identity checks, shift trust to platform gatekeepers, and entrench technical controls that align with the interests of the biggest companies in the stack.

That is why the story resonates so strongly with the LWN audience. For Linux and free-software people, this is not just about social media policy. It is about whether “child safety” becomes the banner under which operating systems become more surveilled, more centrally controlled, and less open.
